does: This paper investigates performance optimization of the Codex agent loop by adopting WebSocket-based communication channels and implementing connection-scoped caching mechanisms. The authors demonstrate that replacing traditional request-response protocols with persistent WebSocket connections significantly reduces API overhead and latency in agentic workflows. Through detailed benchmarking of the Codex system, they show concrete improvements in model response times and throughput when agents repeatedly interact with external services, providing empirical measurements of latency reduction across different agent task profiles and communication patterns.
connects: This work builds on prior research in agent architectures and API efficiency by addressing a practical bottleneck in systems like Codex that perform iterative tool use and environment interaction. It extends existing work on agentic frameworks—which typically use stateless HTTP requests between agent steps—by applying WebSocket technologies previously validated in real-time systems. The paper responds to growing recognition that agent latency is increasingly constrained by communication overhead rather than model computation, building on foundations laid by prior studies of agent loop performance and the practical challenges of deploying language models in interactive settings.
enables: This optimization framework enables substantially more responsive agentic systems suitable for interactive applications requiring tight agent-environment feedback loops. The WebSocket and caching approach opens pathways for developing agents with tighter interactions with code execution environments, databases, and user-facing interfaces where latency directly impacts user experience. The demonstrated techniques could inform design of next-generation agent frameworks and APIs that support long-running, stateful agent sessions, and may enable new classes of agents requiring real-time responsiveness that were previously impractical due to communication bottlenecks.
